walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Ticket to Ride! Place the North America map board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. This board shows cities connected by colored routes of different lengths. Make sure there is room around the board for card displays and player supplies.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Train Car Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the big deck of one hundred and ten Train Car cards. These come in eight colors plus fourteen wild Locomotive cards. Give them a good shuffle. Place the deck face down near the board, then flip five cards face up in a row next to it. This is the card display that players will draw from during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Now find the thirty Destination Ticket cards. These are the smaller cards showing two cities and a point value. Shuffle them into a separate face-down deck and place it near the board. Do not mix these with the Train Car cards. You will deal some to each player in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Place the Bonus Card
walkthrough.text: Find the Longest Continuous Path bonus card and place it face up near the board where everyone can see it. This card is worth ten bonus points at the end of the game to the player with the longest unbroken chain of trains.

walkthrough.title: Choose Colors and Set Up Trains
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes all forty-five train car pieces in that color, plus the matching scoring marker. Place your scoring marker on the Start space of the scoring track that runs around the edge of the board. Keep your train cars in a pile near you.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal four Train Car cards face down to each player. These are your starting hand. You can look at them but keep them hidden from other players. There is no hand limit in Ticket to Ride, so you can hold as many cards as you want throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Look at your tickets carefully. You must keep at least two of the three. You may keep all three if you want. Return any tickets you do not want to the bottom of the Destination Ticket deck. Keep your tickets secret from other players.

walkthrough.title: Pick the First Player
walkthrough.text: The most experienced traveler goes first. If nobody can agree, just pick randomly. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. Each turn, you will choose exactly one of three possible actions. But we will cover those in the rules section. Let's go!

summary.title: Place the Bonus Card

Worth 10 bonus points at game end

Scoring marker on Start (0) of score track

summary.title: Deal Starting Cards
bullets: Deal 4 Train Car cards to each player
Keep hand hidden — no hand limit

Return unwanted tickets to bottom of deck

summary.title: Pick the First Player
bullets: Most experienced traveler goes first
Play proceeds clockwise
One action per turn: Draw Cards, Claim Route, or Draw Tickets

walkthrough.title: One Action Per Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of three things. You can draw Train Car cards, claim a route on the board, or draw more Destination Tickets. That is it. One action, then it is the next player's turn. The game is simple to learn because of this structure. Let's walk through each action.

walkthrough.title: Action: Draw Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: You draw two Train Car cards on your turn. You can take from the five face-up cards or draw blind from the top of the deck, in any combination. When you take a face-up card, immediately replace it from the deck before making your second draw. There is one exception: if you take a face-up Locomotive, which is the wild card, your turn ends immediately. You do not get a second draw. Blind-drawing a Locomotive is fine and still allows a second draw.

walkthrough.title: The Locomotive Reset Rule
walkthrough.text: If three or more of the five face-up cards are Locomotives at any point, discard all five face-up cards and deal five new ones. This prevents the display from being clogged with expensive wild cards. If the new five cards also have three Locomotives, reset again.

walkthrough.title: Action: Claim a Route
walkthrough.text: To claim a route, choose an unclaimed route between two cities on the board. Play a set of Train Car cards from your hand matching the route's color and length. For example, a four-space blue route requires four blue cards. Locomotives are wild and can substitute for any color. Place one of your train cars on each space of the route and discard the played cards. Score points immediately based on the route's length.

walkthrough.title: Gray Routes and Scoring
walkthrough.text: Gray routes can be claimed with any single color. You still need all cards to match one color, plus any Locomotives you want to add. You cannot mix colors. Scoring is based on route length: one train scores one point, two scores two, three scores four, four scores seven, five scores ten, and six scores fifteen. Move your scoring marker on the track immediately.

walkthrough.title: Double Routes
walkthrough.text: Some cities have two parallel routes connecting them. These are double routes. In a four-player or five-player game, different players may claim one track each. However, no single player can ever claim both tracks of a double route. In a two-player or three-player game, only one of the two tracks may be claimed at all. The other is permanently closed.

walkthrough.title: Action: Draw Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Draw three Destination Tickets from the top of the deck. You must keep at least one. You may keep two or all three. Return any you do not want to the bottom of the deck. Completed tickets add their point value to your score at game end. But incomplete tickets subtract their value. Choose carefully.

walkthrough.title: Destination Tickets Explained
walkthrough.text: Each ticket shows two cities and a point value. If your trains create a continuous path between those two cities by the end of the game, you score those points. If not, you lose those points. The path does not need to be direct. It just needs to be an unbroken chain of your claimed routes from one city to the other. Tickets stay secret until the final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Triggering the End Game
walkthrough.text: The end game triggers when any player finishes their turn with two or fewer train cars remaining in their supply. Once this happens, every player, including the player who triggered it, takes one final turn. Then the game ends and you move to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: This has three parts. First, verify all route points scored during the game. Second, reveal all Destination Tickets. Add points for completed tickets and subtract points for incomplete ones. Third, determine who has the Longest Continuous Path. That player gets the ten-point bonus. If players tie for longest path, all tied players receive the bonus. The player with the most total points wins.

bullets: Choose exactly 1 action: Draw Cards, Claim Route, or Draw Tickets

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Ticket to Ride
walkthrough.text: We will walk through the first several turns with four players. By the end of this tutorial, you will understand drawing cards, claiming routes, managing tickets, and the rhythm of play. All aboard!

walkthrough.title: Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Each player has four Train Car cards and has kept two or three Destination Tickets. Player one has tickets connecting Los Angeles to Miami and Seattle to New York. Both are long routes worth a lot of points, so Player one needs to build a cross-country network.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: They look at the five face-up cards and see two red cards, a blue, a green, and a Locomotive. Player one takes a red card from the display. It is immediately replaced from the deck. Then Player one takes the second red card. Now Player one has a growing set of red cards for a future route claim.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Claiming a Short Route
walkthrough.text: Player two has three blue cards and sees a two-space blue route between two cities on one of their tickets. Player two plays two blue cards, places two train pieces on the route, and scores two points immediately. The scoring marker moves from zero to two. Quick and efficient.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Drawing More Cards
walkthrough.text: Player three needs green cards for a specific route but none are face up. They draw two cards from the top of the deck, hoping to get lucky. One is green, one is yellow. Not perfect, but the green card helps. Drawing blind gives you two cards for sure but with no color control.

walkthrough.title: Player Four: Taking the Locomotive
walkthrough.text: Remember, taking a face-up Locomotive counts as your entire turn. No second draw. But Locomotives are wild and can substitute for any color, making them very flexible. It is often worth the cost.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Drawing Extra Tickets Too Early
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player three gets excited and draws three new Destination Tickets. They keep two of them without thinking carefully. Now Player three has five tickets total and has barely started building routes. Those unfinished tickets will cost points at the end. The lesson: do not draw more tickets until your core network is solid.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Focus on Core Routes
walkthrough.text: Player three takes a breath and maps out the shared corridors across all five tickets. Several tickets overlap through the same cities in the middle of the board. By focusing on those shared connections first, Player three can complete multiple tickets with the same routes. Overlap is the key to managing many tickets.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Burning Locomotives on Easy Routes
walkthrough.text: Player one has three red cards and a Locomotive. They use all four to claim a four-space red route. The route scores seven points, but Player one wasted a Locomotive on a route they could have claimed with just one more red card. Now they have no wild card for the tricky gray six-space route they need later.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save Locomotives for Hard Routes
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are your most flexible resource. Save them for routes where matching colors is difficult, such as long gray routes or colors that are scarce in the display. Spending a Locomotive on a three-space route you could fill with regular cards is almost always a mistake.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Claims a Long Route
walkthrough.text: Player two has been patiently collecting five orange cards over several turns. They play all five to claim a five-space route worth ten points. The scoring marker jumps from two to twelve. Long routes are where the big points live. A single five-space route is worth as much as five one-space routes combined.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Chokepoints
walkthrough.text: Player four has been drawing cards without claiming anything for several turns. Suddenly, Player two claims the only route between two critical cities that Player four needed. Now Player four must find an expensive detour. The lesson: identify which routes are likely to be contested and claim them before they are taken, even if you do not have the optimal cards yet.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Find Alternate Routes
walkthrough.text: Player four studies the board and finds a longer alternate path connecting the same cities. It costs more train cars and more turns, but it still completes the ticket. In Ticket to Ride, blocked routes are frustrating but rarely fatal. There is almost always another way, even if it is less efficient.

walkthrough.title: The Game Continues
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise. Each turn, pick one action: draw cards, claim a route, or draw tickets. As the board fills up, routes get scarcer and the pressure increases. Watch your opponents' train supply. When someone gets low, the end game is near. Make sure your tickets are complete before the final round hits.

walkthrough.title: End Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: Player two finishes a turn with only two trains left. This triggers the end game. Everyone, including Player two, gets one final turn. Player one uses their last turn to claim a short route completing their final ticket. Player three manages to connect two more cities. Player four claims one last route. The game is over.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Everyone reveals their Destination Tickets. Player two completed all three tickets for a big positive swing. Player three only completed three of five, losing points on the two incomplete ones. The Longest Continuous Path goes to Player one with a ten-point bonus. Final totals are calculated, and the winner is announced.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through a complete game of Ticket to Ride! You learned to draw cards efficiently, claim routes for points, manage destination tickets, and handle blocked routes. You also saw why Locomotives should be saved and why drawing too many tickets early can hurt you.

walkthrough.title: Before Your Next Journey
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for route planning and card management strategies.

Second, reference the Appendix for the complete scoring table and route rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Network Before Turn One
walkthrough.text: Before you take your first action, study your Destination Tickets and find the shared corridors. If both tickets pass through Chicago, that is your network hub. Identify two or three key routes that serve multiple tickets and prioritize those. Having a plan before turn one prevents wasted turns drawing cards you do not need.

walkthrough.title: Claim Chokepoints Early
walkthrough.text: Some routes on the board have no alternatives. If there is only one way to connect two critical cities on your ticket, claim that route early, even if it means using a Locomotive or claiming a shorter route than you planned. Getting blocked on a chokepoint can force a long, expensive detour or even make a ticket impossible to complete.

walkthrough.title: Long Routes Are Efficient
walkthrough.text: A six-space route scores fifteen points, while six one-space routes score only six points total. Long routes give disproportionately high points per turn invested. When planning your network, favor routes of length four or longer. They are the best use of your limited train supply and turns.

walkthrough.title: Save Locomotives for Critical Routes
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are wild and incredibly valuable. Do not spend them on routes where you almost have enough matching cards. Save them for long gray routes, contested bottleneck routes, or situations where you cannot find the right color. One well-placed Locomotive can be worth five points or more in saved time.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Clock
walkthrough.text: Monitor how many train cars each player has remaining. When someone gets below ten, the end is near. Make sure your most important tickets are complete before someone triggers the final round. If you have incomplete tickets and the end is approaching, shift from drawing cards to claiming routes immediately.

walkthrough.title: Don't Draw Extra Tickets Too Early
walkthrough.text: Drawing more Destination Tickets is tempting because completed tickets score big points. But incomplete tickets are brutal negative points. Only draw new tickets when your existing network has extra connections available or when your core tickets are already complete. Drawing tickets with ten trains left and no safe routes is a recipe for negative points.

walkthrough.title: Balance Drawing and Claiming
walkthrough.text: A common mistake is drawing cards for too many turns in a row without claiming anything. While you are building a perfect hand, opponents are claiming the routes you need. Alternate between drawing and claiming. A good rhythm is two or three draw turns followed by one or two claim turns. Stay active on the board.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Tickets Secret
walkthrough.text: Your Destination Tickets are hidden information and your biggest advantage. Do not reveal your plans by claiming routes in an obvious pattern toward specific cities. Mix in a few routes that serve your tickets but do not telegraph your final destinations. If opponents figure out your tickets, they can block your key routes.

bullets: Find shared corridors across tickets → network hub
Identify 2-3 key routes serving multiple tickets
Plan first, then draw cards that match your plan

Worth using a Locomotive to secure a chokepoint

summary.title: Long Routes Are Efficient

Favor routes of length 4+ for best points per turn
Disproportionate scoring rewards long routes

summary.title: Save Locomotives for Critical Routes
bullets: Wild cards are most valuable on gray or contested routes
Don't burn on routes you could fill with 1 more draw
1 well-placed Locomotive can save 2-3 turns

<10 trains on anyone → end is near
Shift from drawing to claiming when time is short

summary.title: Balance Drawing and Claiming
bullets: Don't draw for 5+ turns straight — opponents claim your routes

Stay active on the board

summary.title: Keep Your Tickets Secret

Don't claim obvious patterns toward target cities
Mix in routes that disguise your destinations

walkthrough.title: Ticket Overlap Optimization
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players build networks where every route serves multiple tickets. Before claiming anything, map all your ticket paths and find where they share the same corridors. A route through Chicago that helps three tickets is three times as valuable as one that helps only one. This is the single most important strategic concept in Ticket to Ride.

walkthrough.title: Denial Plays
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay attention to where opponents are building and occasionally claim routes to block them. A denial play is most valuable when the route you claim also helps your own network. Pure blocking, where you claim a route you do not need just to hurt someone, is rarely worth the spent cards and trains. But if a route serves you and blocks an opponent, it is a premium play.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Timing Control
walkthrough.text: You control when the game ends by managing your train supply. If you are ahead, push to end the game quickly by claiming routes and depleting your trains. If you are behind, slow down and build quietly. Triggering the end when opponents have incomplete tickets is a devastating move. Count everyone's trains every few turns.

walkthrough.title: Longest Path Planning
walkthrough.text: The ten-point Longest Continuous Path bonus can swing the game. Plan for it from the start by building one long connected chain rather than separate branches. Loops count, so connecting routes in circles adds to your path length. The longest path bonus is essentially a free long ticket with no downside risk.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Ticket Draws
walkthrough.text: Drawing tickets in the late game is a calculated gamble. The best time to draw is when your network already connects many cities. Draw three tickets and you might find that two are already complete, giving you free points. The risk is drawing tickets that require routes you cannot build in the remaining turns. Only attempt this when your existing network is extensive.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: In two and three-player games, the double-route restriction means chokepoints are more severe. Claim critical connections earlier. In four and five-player games, competition is fierce, but both tracks of double routes are available. Adjust your urgency based on player count. More players means you need to claim sooner and can afford less time drawing cards.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: There are twelve cards of each color and fourteen Locomotives. Paying attention to what colors have been played and what remains can inform your drawing strategy. If you have seen eight red cards already, your chances of drawing red from the deck are lower. Similarly, if many Locomotives have been claimed, the remaining deck is less flexible. Use this information to decide between face-up draws and blind draws.

summary.title: Ticket Overlap Optimization
bullets: Build routes serving multiple tickets simultaneously
Map all ticket paths → find shared corridors
Route helping 3 tickets = 3x the value

summary.title: Denial Plays
bullets: Block opponents when the route also helps your network
Pure blocking (no self-benefit) is rarely worth the cost
Best denial: serves you AND blocks an opponent

summary.title: End-Game Timing Control
bullets: Ahead? Push to end quickly by depleting trains
Behind? Slow down and build your network first
Count everyone's trains every few turns

summary.title: Longest Path Planning

Build one long chain rather than branches

summary.title: Card Counting

Track what's been played → inform draw strategy
Many Locomotives gone → deck less flexible

entries.definition: The main deck of one hundred and ten cards. Eight colors with twelve cards each, plus fourteen wild Locomotive cards. Used to claim routes on the board.
The wild card in the Train Car deck. Can substitute for any color when claiming a route. Taking a face-up Locomotive counts as your entire draw action. Fourteen in the deck.
A card showing two cities and a point value. Complete by connecting the cities with your routes for positive points. Incomplete tickets subtract their value. Thirty in the deck.
The numbered track running around the edge of the board. Move your marker forward when you claim routes. Points are scored immediately during the game.
A colored path between two adjacent cities on the board. Claimed by playing matching Train Car cards equal to the route's length. Once claimed, it belongs to that player permanently.
A route with no specified color. Can be claimed with any single set of matching-color cards. Locomotives can fill in as always. No color mixing allowed.
Two parallel routes between the same two cities. In 4-5 player games, different players may claim one track each. In 2-3 player games, only one track can be claimed total.
Points scored immediately when claiming a route. One car: one point. Two cars: two points. Three cars: four points. Four cars: seven points. Five cars: ten points. Six cars: fifteen points.

A ten-point bonus awarded to the player with the longest unbroken chain of claimed routes. Loops are allowed. Cities may be passed through multiple times. Segments cannot be reused. Ties share the bonus.
Five Train Car cards laid face up next to the draw deck. Players can choose from these instead of drawing blind. Cards are replaced immediately when taken. Three or more Locomotives trigger a reset.
When three or more of the five face-up cards are Locomotives, discard all five and deal five new face-up cards. This prevents the display from being dominated by wild cards.
When any player ends their turn with zero, one, or two train cars remaining, the end game begins. Every player, including the trigger player, takes one final turn.
A route with no alternative connections between the same two cities. If an opponent claims it, you must find a longer detour. High-priority claim target.
A city that appears on multiple Destination Tickets. Building routes through your hub cities lets you complete many tickets with shared routes.
When two or more Destination Tickets share the same route segments. Overlapping tickets are the most efficient way to score because one claimed route advances multiple tickets.
One of three possible turn actions. Draw two Train Car cards from the face-up display or top of deck. Taking a face-up Locomotive counts as the full two-card draw.
One of three possible turn actions. Play matching cards to place train cars on a route and score points immediately. Limited to one route per turn.
One of three possible turn actions. Draw three Destination Tickets and keep at least one. Return unwanted tickets to the bottom of the deck.
If players tie on total points, the player with the most completed Destination Tickets wins. If still tied, the player with the Longest Continuous Path wins. If still tied, shared victory.
